summary: Martin Fowler reports on finally seeing Intentional Software's Domain Workbench after years of secretive development, calling it a Language Workbench that enables building comprehensive Domain Specific Languages with projectional editing. The tool allows multiple projections of the same semantic model (XML, custom syntax, Ruby, diagrams) that are all editable and synchronized. A key innovation is combining live execution with program definition, similar to spreadsheets, making it powerful for lay programmers. After a demo at DSL DevCon that drew comparisons to the 'Mother of all Demos,' Fowler cautiously notes that while the technology is impressive, no production systems have been built with it yet, and real validation only comes from deployment.
key_points: The Intentional Domain Workbench is a projectional editing environment that supports multiple synchronized views of the same semantic model, allowing users to switch between XML, custom syntax, diagrams, and code representations
Live execution integrated with program definition—like spreadsheets—may be key to making DSLs accessible to lay programmers
The system handles version control at the tree level rather than text, allowing conflicts to be committed as data structures that can be resolved later by appropriate developers
Representational transformations use many small pipelined transforms rather than monolithic ones, similar to multi-stage compilers
Despite the impressive demo that 'exploded brains' at DSL DevCon, no production systems have been built yet, and real understanding only comes from deploying to production
money_quotes: Only Intentional would make such a release and not bother to tell anyone.
The workbench can handle editable diagrammatic representations.
Combining example execution with program definition is one of the features of spreadsheets - and may be a reason why spreadsheets have become so successful as an environment for LayProgrammers.
Unlike with text files they don't mess up your text - you have a real data structure present, so you can find the conflicts and fix them.
As any agilist knows you never really understand something till you deploy it into production every week.
I don't know whether these workbenches will change the face of programming as we know it, after all I once thought Smalltalk was going to be our future.
Two words to say about the Electronics demo for Intentional Software: HOLY CRAPOLA. That's it, my brain has finally exploded.
This is like seeing a 100-mpg carburetor: OMG someone is going to buy this and put it in a vault!
key_insight: Language workbenches that combine live execution with projectional editing across multiple synchronized representations have transformative potential, but impressive demos must be validated through real production deployment.
